text: A model with high {{c1::bias}} underfits the data, while high {{c2::variance}} overfits and fails to generalize.
explanation: Bias-variance tradeoff is a core concept for choosing models, regularization, and validation strategies.
usage: Fundamental for supervised learning, model selection, and debugging poor performance.
spanish: Un modelo con alto sesgo subajusta los datos, mientras que alta varianza sobreajusta y falla en generalizar.

text: Cross-validation estimates generalization by repeatedly splitting data into train and validation {{c1::folds}}.
explanation: It reduces the risk of reporting optimistic performance from a single lucky split.
usage: Used in model comparison, hyperparameter tuning, and feature engineering workflows.
spanish: La validación cruzada estima la generalización al dividir los datos en múltiples folds de entrenamiento y validación.

text: A {{c1::confounder}} can create a misleading association between a feature and the target if it is not controlled.
explanation: Confounding is central for causal reasoning and for avoiding false conclusions in experimentation.
usage: Important in observational studies, marketing analysis, and policy evaluation.
spanish: Un factor de confusión puede crear una asociación engañosa entre una característica y la meta si no se controla.

text: {{c1::Regularization}} adds a penalty to limit model complexity and reduce overfitting.
explanation: L1 and L2 regularization are standard tools for controlling variance in linear and neural models.
usage: Essential in logistic regression, SVMs, and deep learning optimization.
spanish: La regularización añade una penalización para limitar la complejidad del modelo y reducir el sobreajuste.

text: In imbalanced datasets, metrics like {{c1::precision}}, {{c2::recall}}, and {{c3::F1}} are often more informative than raw accuracy.
explanation: Class imbalance can make accuracy nearly useless when one class dominates the dataset.
usage: Common in fraud detection, rare disease prediction, and anomaly detection.
spanish: En conjuntos con clases desbalanceadas, métricas como precisión, recall y F1 suelen ser más informativas que la exactitud pura.

text: Feature engineering transforms raw {{c1::variables}} into representations that expose relevant structure to the model.
explanation: Good features often matter more than the algorithm itself in practical forecasting and tabular problems.
usage: Central in credit scoring, churn prediction, and recommendation systems.
spanish: La ingeniería de características transforma variables crudas en representaciones que exponen estructura relevante al modelo.

text: A calibrated model outputs probabilities that reflect {{c1::real}}-world confidence rather than just ranking examples.
explanation: Calibration is essential for risk estimation, medical diagnosis, and decision support.
usage: Used when probabilities matter more than hard labels.
spanish: Un modelo calibrado devuelve probabilidades que reflejan la confianza real en lugar de solo ordenar ejemplos.

text: {{c1::Distribution shift}} occurs when the data seen in production no longer matches the training distribution.
explanation: This is one of the most common causes of degraded real-world ML performance.
usage: Critical for deployment monitoring, forecasting, and fraud systems.
spanish: El cambio de distribución ocurre cuando los datos vistos en producción ya no coinciden con la distribución de entrenamiento.

scenario: Dual-Coding Diagram 📊 (Word2Vec CBOW vs. Skip-gram (Mikolov et al., 2013))
text: ¿Cómo funciona la estructura de {{c1::Word2Vec CBOW vs. Skip-gram (Mikolov et al., 2013)}} en el sistema?
explanation:
CBOW predicts the current target word from surrounding context words, making it faster to train on frequent words. Skip-gram predicts the surrounding context words given the current target word, performing better with small datasets and representing rare words well.

graph TD subgraph CBOW ["Continuous Bag of Words (CBOW)"] Context["Context words: x(t-2), x(t-1), x(t+1), x(t+2)"] --> Projection["Sum / Average projection layer"] Projection --> Target["Target word: y(t)"] end subgraph Skipgram ["Skip-gram"] Input["Input word: x(t)"] --> Projection_SG["Projection layer"] Projection_SG --> Output1["Target Context: y(t-2)"] Projection_SG --> Output2["Target Context: y(t-1)"] Projection_SG --> Output3["Target Context: y(t+1)"] Projection_SG --> Output4["Target Context: y(t+2)"] end
usage:
Visual model for Word2Vec CBOW vs. Skip-gram (Mikolov et al., 2013).
Graph:
graph TD subgraph CBOW ["Continuous Bag of Words (CBOW)"] Context["Context words: x(t-2), x(t-1), x(t+1), x(t+2)"] --> Projection["Sum / Average projection layer"] Projection --> Target["Target word: y(t)"] end subgraph Skipgram ["Skip-gram"] Input["Input word: x(t)"] --> Projection_SG["Projection layer"] Projection_SG --> Output1["Target Context: y(t-2)"] Projection_SG --> Output2["Target Context: y(t-1)"] Projection_SG --> Output3["Target Context: y(t+1)"] Projection_SG --> Output4["Target Context: y(t+2)"] end
spanish: CBOW predice la palabra objetivo actual basándose en las palabras de contexto vecinas, mientras que Skip-gram predice el contexto vecino dada la palabra objetivo actual.

text: Ecuación para {{c1::Word2Vec Negative Sampling Loss}}: \[\mathcal{L}_{\text{NEG}}(v_{w_I}, v'_{w_O}, \mathcal{W}_{\text{neg}}) = -\log \sigma(v'_{w_O}{}^\top v_{w_I}) - \sum_{w_i \in \mathcal{W}_{\text{neg}}} \log \sigma(-v'_{w_i}{}^\top v_{w_I})\]
explanation:
Desglose de variables:
  • \(v_{w_I}\): Input vector representation of the current word.
  • \(v'_{w_O}\): Output vector representation of the true context word.
  • \(\mathcal{W}_{\text{neg}}\): Set of \(k\) randomly sampled negative noise words.
  • \(\sigma\): Sigmoid activation function.
spanish: Fórmula de Word2Vec Negative Sampling Loss

text: Ecuación para {{c1::Hierarchical Softmax Approximation}}: \[P(w|w_I) = \prod_{j=1}^{L(w)-1} \sigma\left([[\text{ch}(n(w, j)) == n(w, j+1)]] \cdot v'_{n(w, j)}{}^\top v_{w_I}\right)\]
explanation:
Desglose de variables:
  • \(L(w)\): Depth of word \(w\) in the binary Huffman tree.
  • \(n(w, j)\): The \(j\)-th node along the path from root to word \(w\).
  • \(\text{ch}(n)\): Left child of node \(n\).
  • \([[\cdot]]\): Evaluates to \(1\) if true, \(-1\) if false.
  • \(v'_{n(w, j)}\): Vector representation of internal node \(n(w, j)\).
usage: Formula: \[P(w|w_I) = \prod_{j=1}^{L(w)-1} \sigma\left([[\text{ch}(n(w, j)) == n(w, j+1)]] \cdot v'_{n(w, j)}{}^\top v_{w_I}\right)\]

scenario: Math & Physics 📐 (GloVe Optimization Objective (Pennington et al., 2014))
text: Ecuación para {{c1::GloVe Optimization Objective (Pennington et al., 2014)}}: \[J = \sum_{i,j=1}^V f(X_{i,j}) \left( w_i^\top \tilde{w}_j + b_i + \tilde{b}_j - \log X_{i,j} \right)^2\]
explanation:
Desglose de variables:
  • \(V\): Vocabulary size.
  • \(X_{i,j}\): Co-occurrence count between word \(i\) and word \(j\).
  • \(w_i, \tilde{w}_j\): Word vectors for target word \(i\) and context word \(j\).
  • \(b_i, \tilde{b}_j\): Biases for word \(i\) and context word \(j\).
  • \(f(X_{i,j})\): Weighting function to cap very high co-occurrences.
spanish: Fórmula de GloVe Optimization Objective (Pennington et al., 2014)

scenario: Math & Physics 📐 (VC Dimension Sample Complexity Bound)
text: Ecuación para {{c1::VC Dimension Sample Complexity Bound}}: \[m \ge C \left( \frac{d + \log(1/\delta)}{\epsilon^2} \right)\]
explanation:
Desglose de variables:
  • \(m\): The sample size required to guarantee PAC (Probably Approximately Correct) learning.
  • \(d\): The Vapnik-Chervonenkis (VC) dimension of the hypothesis class, representing its capacity.
  • \(\delta\): The confidence parameter (generalization failure probability is at most \(\delta\)).
  • \(\epsilon\): The generalization error tolerance.
  • \(C\): A universal constant.
spanish: Fórmula de VC Dimension Sample Complexity Bound
